questions.question: Define force.
questions.answer: A force is a push or pull applied on an object resulting from its interaction with another object.
questions.solution: A force is fundamentally defined as a push or pull. In physics, forces arise only through the interaction between two objects. For example, when you push a door, your hand (one object) and the door (another object) interact, and a force is exerted. The SI unit of force is the newton (N), named after Sir Isaac Newton.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.answer: The SI unit of force is newton, symbolized as N.
questions.solution: The SI (International System of Units) uses newton (N) as the standard unit for measuring force. This unit is named after Isaac Newton, the famous scientist who formulated laws of motion and gravity. One newton is defined as the force required to accelerate a mass of 1 kilogram at 1 meter per second squared.

questions.question: Name three effects that a force can produce on an object.
questions.answer: Force can: (1) make an object move from rest, (2) change the speed of a moving object, and (3) change the direction of motion.
questions.solution: When force is applied to an object, it can produce several distinct effects: (1) Make an object move from rest—applying push or pull on a stationary object initiates motion. (2) Change the speed of a moving object—force can increase speed (acceleration) or decrease speed (deceleration). (3) Change the direction of motion—force can alter the path of an object without changing its speed. Additionally, force can also change the shape of an object (like pressing a balloon) or cause a combination of these effects simultaneously.

questions.question: What is muscular force?
questions.answer: Muscular force is the force resulting from the contraction and elongation of muscles in the body.
questions.solution: Muscular force is generated when muscles contract (shorten) and elongate (stretch) during physical activities. This force enables all physical movements in living organisms, including walking, running, lifting, pushing, jumping, and stretching in humans. All animals, birds, fish, and insects use muscular forces for movement and survival. Historically, humans also utilized the muscular force of animals like horses and oxen for transportation and agricultural work.

questions.question: What is friction?
questions.answer: Friction is a contact force that arises when an object moves or tries to move over another surface, acting opposite to the direction of motion.
questions.solution: Friction is a contact force that always opposes motion. When an object moves or attempts to move across a surface, friction acts in the direction opposite to the intended motion. For example, when you push a box on a table, friction acts backward. Friction arises due to irregularities (roughness) in the surfaces in contact—even smooth-looking surfaces have microscopic irregularities that lock together and resist motion. Rough surfaces produce greater friction than smooth surfaces.

questions.question: Name three examples of contact forces.
questions.answer: Muscular force, friction, and the force of your hand pushing an object.
questions.solution: Contact forces require physical contact between two objects. Examples include: (1) Muscular force—pushing, pulling, or lifting using body muscles. (2) Friction—the force that stops a sliding object. (3) Pushing or pulling with hands or tools. (4) A ball hitting a bat and changing direction. (5) Pressing a balloon and deforming it. All these require direct physical contact between the interacting objects.

questions.question: What is magnetic force?
questions.answer: Magnetic force is the force exerted by a magnet on another magnet or a magnetic material, acting from a distance without physical contact.
questions.solution: Magnetic force is a non-contact force exerted by magnets. Key characteristics: (1) It acts without requiring physical contact. (2) Like poles (North-North or South-South) repel each other. (3) Unlike poles (North-South) attract each other. (4) A magnet attracts objects made of magnetic materials like iron, nickel, and cobalt. (5) Electromagnets (coils carrying electric current) also produce magnetic forces. These properties make magnetic force a powerful non-contact force used in many applications.

questions.question: What is electrostatic force?
questions.answer: Electrostatic force is the force exerted by a charged body on another charged or uncharged body, acting without physical contact.
questions.solution: Electrostatic force results from static electric charges. When two objects of certain materials are rubbed together, electrical charges build up on their surfaces. These stationary charges are called static charges. The charged objects then exert force on other charged or uncharged objects from a distance (non-contact force). Key rules: (1) Like charges (both positive or both negative) repel each other. (2) Unlike charges (positive and negative) attract each other. (3) A charged object attracts uncharged objects made of certain materials like paper pieces.

questions.question: What is gravitational force?
questions.answer: Gravitational force is the force with which the Earth attracts all objects towards itself.
questions.solution: Gravitational force is the force of attraction between the Earth and all objects. Key characteristics: (1) It is a non-contact force—acts without touching the object. (2) Always attractive—never repulsive. (3) Acts on all objects, pulling them toward the Earth. (4) This is why thrown objects fall back down, why we stay on the ground, and why objects have weight. (5) The force is proportional to the object's mass—heavier objects experience stronger gravitational pull. Gravitational force is the force responsible for the weight of objects.

questions.question: Define weight.
questions.answer: Weight is the gravitational force with which the Earth pulls an object towards itself. SI unit: newton (N).
questions.solution: Weight is specifically the gravitational force acting on an object due to Earth's pull. Unlike mass (which is fixed everywhere), weight can vary. Key points: (1) Measured in newtons (N), not kilograms. (2) Measured using a spring balance. (3) Different objects have different weights—Earth pulls them with different forces based on their mass. (4) Weight depends on location—it can vary slightly from place to place on Earth. (5) Weight is very different on other planets due to different gravitational strengths.

questions.question: What is the difference between mass and weight?
questions.answer: Mass is the amount of matter in an object (measured in kg, constant everywhere). Weight is the gravitational force on an object (measured in N, varies with location and planet).
questions.solution: These are fundamentally different concepts often confused in everyday language: Mass: (1) Amount of matter in an object. (2) Measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg). (3) Remains constant everywhere—same on Earth, Moon, or Mars. (4) Measured using a beam balance or by calculation. Weight: (1) Gravitational force exerted by Earth (or another planet) on an object. (2) Measured in newtons (N). (3) Varies with location and planet. (4) Measured using a spring balance. Example: A 1 kg object has constant mass of 1 kg everywhere. On Earth, its weight is ~10 N. On the Moon (weaker gravity), its weight is only ~1.6 N. On Jupiter (stronger gravity), its weight is ~25.4 N.

questions.question: What is a spring balance and what does it measure?
questions.answer: A spring balance is a device with a spring and hook used to measure the weight of objects. The spring stretches according to the weight, and a scale shows the reading in newtons.
questions.solution: A spring balance consists of: (1) A spring fixed at one end. (2) A hook at the other end for hanging objects. (3) A graduated scale marked in newtons (N) to indicate weight. (4) Usually also shows corresponding mass values in grams. How it works: When an object is hung from the hook, the spring stretches proportionally to the object's weight. The amount of stretching indicates the force (weight) acting on the spring. The scale is calibrated assuming use on Earth with constant gravitational force. This device is simple, portable, and widely used in schools for weight measurements.

questions.question: What is upthrust or buoyant force?
questions.answer: Upthrust (buoyant force) is the upward force applied by a liquid on an object placed in it.
questions.solution: When an object is placed in a liquid, the liquid exerts an upward force on the object called upthrust or buoyant force. Evidence: When you push a bottle (sealed with water) into water, you feel an upward push. When released, it bounces back up. This upward force acts opposite to gravitational force (which acts downward). The strength of buoyant force depends partly on the density of the liquid—denser liquids provide stronger buoyant force. Buoyant force is essential for understanding why some objects float while others sink in water.

questions.question: When does an object float and when does it sink?
questions.answer: An object floats if buoyant force equals or exceeds its weight. An object sinks if its weight exceeds the buoyant force.
questions.solution: Two forces act on an object in a liquid: (1) Gravitational force (weight) acting downward. (2) Buoyant force (upthrust) acting upward. Three outcomes: If Weight > Buoyant Force: Gravitational force is stronger, so the object sinks to the bottom. If Weight = Buoyant Force: Forces balance, so the object floats at the surface or remains suspended. If Weight < Buoyant Force: Buoyant force is stronger, so the object rises and floats on the surface. The comparison of these two forces determines the object's behavior in the liquid. For example, a heavy stone sinks (weight > buoyant force), while a wooden block floats (weight < buoyant force).

questions.question: Explain why friction is important in everyday life and give two examples where it is necessary.
questions.answer: Friction enables us to walk, grip objects, and stop vehicles. Without friction, surfaces would be slippery. Examples: (1) Walking requires friction between shoes and ground. (2) Brakes require friction to stop vehicles.
questions.solution: Friction, while sometimes seen as an obstacle, is actually essential for many daily activities: Why friction is important: (1) Enables movement—without friction, we couldn't walk or run as our feet would slip. (2) Enables gripping—hands can hold objects due to friction between hand and object. (3) Enables stopping—brakes work by friction between brake pads and wheels. (4) Prevents sliding—rough surfaces have more friction and are safer. Examples where friction is necessary: (1) Walking: Friction between shoes and ground prevents slipping and enables forward motion. (2) Vehicle braking: Friction between brake pads and brake discs creates the force that stops vehicles. (3) Writing: Friction between pen and paper allows ink transfer. (4) Climbing: Friction between hands/feet and surface prevents falling. Without friction in these situations, many basic activities would be impossible or extremely dangerous.

questions.question: Two balloons are rubbed with a woollen cloth. What happens when they are brought near each other and why?
questions.answer: The two balloons repel each other because both acquire the same type of charge (from the same woollen cloth) and like charges repel.
questions.solution: This is a classic electrostatic force demonstration: What happens: When both balloons are rubbed with the same woollen cloth, both acquire static charges. When brought near each other without touching, the balloons move away from each other as if repelling. Why this happens: (1) Both balloons acquire the same type of electric charge—either both become positively charged or both become negatively charged. (2) According to the law of electrostatic forces: Like charges (same type) repel each other. (3) Unlike charges (opposite types) attract each other. (4) In this experiment, the similar charges on both balloons repel, causing them to move apart. If instead we bring the woollen cloth (which has opposite charge) near one balloon, they would attract each other, demonstrating that unlike charges attract. This demonstrates the non-contact nature of electrostatic force—the balloons repel without touching.

questions.question: Explain why the speed of a moving object decreases when rolling on rough ground compared to smooth ground.
questions.answer: Rough ground produces greater friction than smooth ground. Greater friction causes more deceleration, so the object stops sooner.
questions.solution: Friction is the force responsible for the decrease in speed: Speed decrease mechanism: (1) Friction acts opposite to the direction of motion. (2) This opposing force causes the object's speed to decrease (deceleration). (3) Eventually, friction brings the object to a complete stop. Why rough surfaces produce more deceleration: (1) Friction arises from irregularities (roughness) in the contact surfaces. (2) Rough surfaces have larger irregularities that lock together more effectively. (3) This creates greater resistance to motion, resulting in stronger friction force. (4) Stronger friction causes faster deceleration and the object stops sooner. Comparison: A ball rolling on smooth glass experiences less friction and travels farther before stopping. The same ball on rough sand experiences greater friction and stops much sooner. This principle is why sandy beaches slow down vehicles while smooth roads allow them to travel farther. The nature of the surface directly determines friction strength.

questions.question: Explain what happens when you throw a ball vertically upwards. Describe the motion and forces involved.
questions.answer: The ball moves upward, slows down, stops momentarily at the top, then falls back down. Throughout, gravitational force acts downward opposite to the initial velocity.
questions.solution: Throwing a ball vertically upward demonstrates gravitational force clearly: Stages of motion: (1) Upward motion: The ball moves upward with initial velocity. Gravitational force acts downward (opposite to motion), causing the ball to decelerate. The speed decreases continuously. (2) At the top: The ball momentarily stops—velocity becomes zero, but gravitational force still acts downward. (3) Downward motion: The ball falls downward. Gravitational force acts downward (same as direction of motion), causing acceleration. The speed increases continuously. Force analysis: (1) Throughout all stages, gravitational force acts downward—pulling the ball toward Earth. (2) During upward motion, gravity opposes motion (acts backward), causing deceleration. (3) During downward motion, gravity aids motion (acts forward), causing acceleration. (4) At the top, gravity acts downward, changing the direction of velocity from upward to downward. This example clearly demonstrates: (1) Gravitational force is always present and always acts downward. (2) Force can change both the speed and direction of motion. (3) An object in flight experiences continuous gravitational pull throughout its path.

questions.question: A 1 kg object has a weight of 10 N on Earth. What would be its weight on the Moon where gravitational force is one-sixth that of Earth?
questions.answer: Weight on Moon = 10 N ÷ 6 = 1.6 N. The mass remains 1 kg (unchanged), but weight becomes 1.6 N.
questions.solution: This question demonstrates the difference between mass and weight: Given information: Mass = 1 kg (same everywhere) Weight on Earth = 10 N Gravitational force on Moon = 1/6 of Earth's gravitational force Calculation: Weight is proportional to gravitational force: Weight on Moon = Weight on Earth × (Moon's gravity / Earth's gravity) Weight on Moon = 10 N × (1/6) = 10/6 = 1.67 N ≈ 1.6 N Key insights: (1) Mass remains constant at 1 kg—mass does not change with location. (2) Weight changes because gravitational force is different on the Moon. (3) The Moon's weaker gravity means everything weighs less on the Moon. (4) An astronaut weighing 700 N on Earth would weigh only ~117 N on the Moon—six times lighter! This illustrates why astronauts feel 'lighter' on the Moon and can jump much higher, even though their mass (amount of matter) is exactly the same.

questions.question: How should you read a spring balance to get an accurate weight measurement?
questions.answer: Identify the range and smallest division value. Hang the object carefully and read the scale where the pointer aligns, ensuring eye level reading.
questions.solution: Reading a spring balance accurately involves several steps: Step 1 - Understand the scale: (1) Look at the spring balance carefully. (2) Identify the maximum weight it can measure (its range). (3) Note the range—for example, 0 to 10 N. Step 2 - Calculate smallest division value: (1) Find the weight difference between two consecutive bigger marks. (2) Count the number of smaller divisions between them. (3) Calculate: Smallest division = Weight difference ÷ Number of divisions. Example: If there is 1 N between two bigger marks and 5 smaller divisions between them, each small division = 1 N ÷ 5 = 0.2 N. Step 3 - Measure the object: (1) Ensure the object's weight does not exceed the balance's maximum. (2) Hang the object carefully from the hook. (3) Allow the spring to stabilize. (4) Read the scale at eye level where the pointer points. (5) If the pointer is between marks, estimate using the smallest division. Step 4 - Record the reading: Write down the weight value in newtons with appropriate precision. Important: Always examine the instrument before use—different balances have different ranges and division values.

questions.question: Explain why a charged plastic scale attracts small pieces of paper without touching them.
questions.answer: The rubbed plastic scale acquires static charges. These charges create an electrostatic field that exerts attractive force on the paper pieces (non-contact force).
questions.solution: This experiment demonstrates electrostatic force: Process: (1) When a plastic scale is rubbed vigorously with polythene, static charges build up on the scale's surface. (2) The scale becomes a charged object. (3) When brought near small pieces of paper (without touching), the paper pieces are attracted and stick to the scale. Why attraction occurs: (1) The charged scale creates an electrostatic field around it. (2) This field exerts force on the uncharged paper pieces. (3) The charged scale and neutral paper are attracted to each other. (4) This is a non-contact force—no physical contact is needed. Characteristics of electrostatic force: (1) Acts over distance without requiring contact. (2) Can attract uncharged objects made of certain materials (like paper). (3) Stronger attraction occurs with opposite charges. (4) Can also cause repulsion between similarly charged objects. This demonstrates that charges are real physical properties that create forces at a distance, explaining many phenomena like lightning and the behavior of static electricity in daily life.

questions.question: A wooden block floats in water while an iron nail sinks. Explain this difference using the concept of buoyant force.
questions.answer: Both experience downward gravitational force and upward buoyant force. For the nail, weight > buoyant force (sinks). For the block, weight ≤ buoyant force (floats).
questions.solution: This question requires comparing forces for two objects in water: For the iron nail (sinks): (1) Gravitational force (weight) = Large (iron is dense and heavy). (2) Buoyant force = Smaller value (nail displaces small volume of water). (3) Result: Weight > Buoyant force, so the net downward force exceeds buoyant force. (4) The nail sinks to the bottom. For the wooden block (floats): (1) Gravitational force (weight) = Smaller (wood is less dense than water). (2) Buoyant force = Larger or equal value (block displaces significant water volume). (3) Result: Weight ≤ Buoyant force, so the net forces balance or buoyant force exceeds weight. (4) The block floats on the surface or remains suspended. Key principle: When an object is in a liquid, two opposite forces determine its behavior: Downward: Gravitational force (weight of object). Upward: Buoyant force (equal to weight of water displaced). The net effect: If Weight > Buoyant Force → Object sinks. If Weight = Buoyant Force → Object floats. If Weight < Buoyant Force → Object rises. Material density is the underlying factor: Denser materials (like iron) are heavier for their volume, so weight exceeds buoyant force. Less dense materials (like wood) are lighter for their volume, so buoyant force is sufficient to support them.

questions.question: A ball is thrown horizontally from the top of a building and follows a curved path. Explain the forces acting on the ball and how they affect its motion throughout the flight.
questions.answer: Gravitational force acts downward throughout the flight. It has no component in the horizontal direction, so it doesn't affect horizontal motion but continuously increases vertical velocity.
questions.solution: This analysis requires understanding how gravity affects motion in two dimensions: Initial conditions: The ball is thrown horizontally, so initial velocity is horizontal and zero vertical velocity. Forces during flight: (1) Gravitational force: Always acts vertically downward (constant throughout flight). (2) Air resistance: Typically ignored in simple problems; we assume negligible. (3) No horizontal force: Nothing acts to change horizontal velocity. Motion analysis: Horizontal motion: (1) Initial horizontal velocity is constant. (2) Gravitational force acts vertically, not horizontally. (3) No horizontal force means horizontal velocity remains constant throughout flight. (4) Ball travels equal horizontal distances in equal time intervals. Vertical motion: (1) Initially, vertical velocity is zero. (2) Gravitational force acts downward, causing downward acceleration. (3) Vertical velocity increases continuously as the ball falls. (4) Distance fallen increases with time (not uniformly, but according to motion equations). Resulting trajectory: (1) The combination of constant horizontal velocity and increasing vertical velocity creates a curved path. (2) Initially, the path is relatively flat (small vertical velocity). (3) As time passes, the path becomes steeper (large vertical velocity). (4) The shape is parabolic. Key insights: (1) Gravitational force affects only vertical motion, not horizontal motion. (2) The horizontal component of velocity remains constant. (3) The vertical component continuously increases due to gravity. (4) This demonstrates that force changes velocity but not in all directions equally. This example shows the profound effect of gravitational force in determining projectile motion—a fundamental concept in physics.

questions.question: Explain why ships and aeroplanes are designed with streamlined shapes. Relate this to friction acting on them.
questions.answer: Streamlined shapes reduce the contact area and minimize irregularities between the object surface and fluid (water/air), thereby reducing friction forces acting on them.
questions.solution: Streamlined design is directly related to reducing fluid friction: Nature of the problem: (1) Ships move through water, aeroplanes move through air. (2) Both water and air exert friction forces on moving objects. (3) Friction opposes motion and requires energy to overcome. (4) Reducing friction makes movement more efficient and faster. How friction acts in fluids: (1) Friction between a solid object and flowing fluid depends on: Surface area in contact with the fluid. Nature and smoothness of the surface. Speed of movement. Density of the fluid. (2) Larger contact area = Greater friction. (3) Rougher surfaces = Greater friction. (4) The fluid molecules interact with the object's surface, creating resistance. Streamlined design solutions: (1) Pointed front (bow/nose): Reduces the area that directly pushes through the fluid, minimizing frontal resistance. (2) Tapered rear (stern/tail): Allows the fluid to flow smoothly behind the object without creating turbulence. (3) Smooth surfaces: Reduces irregularities that increase friction. (4) Rounded curves: Allows smooth fluid flow along the surface instead of creating chaotic turbulence. Benefits of streamlining: (1) Reduced friction means less resistance to motion. (2) Less energy needed to maintain speed or accelerate. (3) Higher maximum speeds become achievable. (4) Better fuel efficiency. (5) Smoother, more stable movement. Examples: (1) Submarines are completely streamlined to move efficiently underwater. (2) High-speed trains have pointed fronts to reduce air resistance. (3) Fish are naturally streamlined—an evolutionary adaptation for efficient swimming. This demonstrates a practical application of understanding friction forces in engineering and design.

questions.question: Three objects of the same size but different densities are placed in water. They dip to different depths. Relate this to gravitational and buoyant forces.
questions.answer: Objects with higher weight (greater mass) need larger buoyant force to float. If buoyant force equals weight, object floats deeper. Different densities result in different weight-to-volume ratios, causing different immersion depths.
questions.solution: This problem requires understanding how buoyant force varies with immersion depth: Key principle: Buoyant force equals the weight of liquid displaced. As an object sinks deeper, it displaces more liquid, increasing buoyant force. Three objects scenario: Let's say three objects: Object 1 (high density): (1) High weight (due to high density material). (2) Needs large buoyant force to balance weight. (3) Must displace large volume of water to achieve sufficient buoyant force. (4) Result: Sinks deeply into the water, nearly submerged. Object 2 (medium density): (1) Medium weight. (2) Needs moderate buoyant force. (3) Partially immersed, displacing moderate volume. Object 3 (low density): (1) Low weight (less dense material). (2) Needs only small buoyant force to balance weight. (3) Displaces small volume of water. (4) Result: Barely submerged, mostly floating on surface. Mathematical relationship: At equilibrium (floating): Weight of object = Weight of water displaced (Archimedes' Principle). For objects of same size but different densities: Density₁ > Density₂ > Density₃ Therefore: Weight₁ > Weight₂ > Weight₃ Volume displaced: V₁ > V₂ > V₃ (proportional to weight) Immersion depth: d₁ > d₂ > d₃ (proportional to weight) Analysis of forces: (1) Gravitational force (weight) acting downward is determined by mass and density. (2) Buoyant force acting upward increases as more volume is immersed. (3) At equilibrium, these two forces balance. (4) Objects with higher weight achieve balance at greater immersion depths. Real-world application: This principle explains why: (1) Heavy ships sit lower in water than light boats. (2) Submarines can control depth by adjusting weight. (3) Icebergs float with mostly submerged parts because ice is less dense than water. (4) Different materials behave differently in water based on their density-to-size ratio.

questions.question: A ball rolling on a surface gradually slows down and stops. Identify all forces acting on the ball and explain how they cause it to stop.
questions.answer: Gravitational force acts downward (balanced by normal force from surface). Friction acts opposite to motion, opposing the ball's movement and causing deceleration until it stops.
questions.solution: Analyzing forces on a rolling ball provides insight into real-world friction: Forces acting on the ball: (1) Gravitational force: Acts downward, pulling the ball toward Earth's center. (2) Normal force: Acts upward from the surface, perpendicular to the ground, balancing gravitational force. (3) Friction force: Acts horizontally opposite to the direction of motion, resisting the ball's movement. (4) Air resistance: Small force opposing motion through air (often neglected in simple problems). Force analysis: Vertical direction: (1) Gravitational force (downward) = Weight of ball. (2) Normal force (upward) = Equal to gravitational force. (3) These vertical forces balance, so no motion occurs vertically. The ball stays on the surface. Horizontal direction: (1) Initially, the ball has horizontal velocity (from the push). (2) Friction acts horizontally opposite to velocity. (3) Friction is the only unbalanced horizontal force. (4) According to Newton's laws, this unbalanced force causes deceleration. (5) The ball's velocity decreases over time. (6) Eventually, velocity becomes zero and the ball stops. Why the ball stops: (1) Friction continuously opposes motion. (2) Friction force is unbalanced (no opposing horizontal force). (3) The unbalanced friction force causes negative acceleration (deceleration). (4) Velocity decreases linearly: v = v₀ - (friction/mass) × time. (5) When v = 0, the ball stops. (6) Once stopped, static friction prevents further motion. Factors affecting stopping distance: (1) Initial velocity: Higher initial speed means greater distance before stopping. (2) Friction coefficient: Higher friction (rougher surface) causes shorter stopping distance. (3) Mass: Heavier balls experience same friction force, but mass affects acceleration (F=ma). (4) Surface material: Different surfaces have different friction coefficients. Applications in real life: (1) Braking systems use friction to stop vehicles. (2) Anti-lock braking systems optimize friction for safety. (3) Sports surfaces are designed with appropriate friction for safety and performance. (4) Understanding friction is crucial for vehicle safety calculations.

questions.question: Explain why we cannot use a spring balance designed for use on Earth to accurately measure weight on the Moon without modification.
questions.answer: A spring balance is calibrated for Earth's gravity. On the Moon (1/6 gravity), objects weigh 1/6 less, so the spring extends 1/6 the distance, giving wrong readings on the Earth-calibrated scale.
questions.solution: This question explores how instruments are location-dependent: How a spring balance works: (1) Spring balance uses Hooke's Law: Spring force = k × extension, where k is spring constant. (2) At equilibrium, spring force equals object's weight. (3) Extension = Weight / k. (4) The scale markings are calibrated such that each extension corresponds to a specific weight in newtons. Calibration for Earth: (1) Spring balance is calibrated assuming g ≈ 10 m/s² (Earth's gravity). (2) For a 1 kg object on Earth: Weight = 1 kg × 10 m/s² = 10 N. (3) The spring extends by: Extension = 10 N / k. (4) The scale marks this extension as '10 N' (or sometimes shows '1 kg'). On the Moon without modification: (1) Moon's gravity = 1/6 of Earth's gravity. (2) Same 1 kg object on Moon: Weight = 1 kg × (10/6) m/s² ≈ 1.6 N. (3) Spring extends by: Extension = 1.6 N / k. (4) BUT the scale (calibrated for Earth) marks this extension as '1.6 N' instead of the actual '10 N'. (5) Reading error: Scale shows 1.6 N; actual weight is 1.6 N (coincidentally correct in this case). Actually, let's reconsider: The scale markings are calibrated for Earth such that: 1 cm extension = 10 N on Earth. On the Moon, the same object stretching the spring 1 cm (if it could be stretched equally) would indicate 10 N on the scale, but the actual weight is 1.6 N. So the scale would give incorrect readings. Why modification is needed: (1) The spring constant k of the spring doesn't change. (2) But the relationship between weight and extension does change because gravity is different. (3) A new scale calibration is needed for Moon's gravity. (4) Markings would need to show that 1 cm extension = ~1.6 N instead of 10 N. Solutions: (1) Create a new scale calibrated for Moon's gravity. (2) Use a different spring with different spring constant. (3) Use an electronic scale that calculates weight from gravitational acceleration. (4) Use Archimedes' principle with water displacement instead. General principle: (1) Any measurement of weight is location-dependent. (2) Instruments calibrated for one location give wrong results at another location with different gravity. (3) This is why precise measurements must account for local gravity values. (4) Astronauts on different celestial bodies need appropriately calibrated equipment.

questions.question: A person stands on the edge of a cliff and throws a stone horizontally at high speed. Another person drops a stone vertically downward. Which stone reaches the ground first and why?
questions.answer: Both stones reach the ground at the same time because gravitational force acts equally on both, causing the same vertical acceleration regardless of horizontal motion.
questions.solution: This is a classic physics problem demonstrating independence of horizontal and vertical motion: Setup: (1) Both stones start at the same height (edge of cliff). (2) Stone A: Thrown horizontally with high speed. (3) Stone B: Dropped vertically (zero horizontal velocity). (4) Both experience only gravitational force (ignoring air resistance). Analysis of motion: Vertical motion (determines when stone reaches ground): (1) Both stones experience identical gravitational force. (2) Both have initial vertical velocity = 0. (3) Both undergo identical vertical acceleration = g ≈ 10 m/s². (4) Using kinematics: h = ½gt², where h is height and t is time. (5) Solving for time: t = √(2h/g). (6) This time is INDEPENDENT of horizontal velocity. (7) Therefore, both stones take the same time to reach the ground. Horizontal motion (affects landing position, not time): (1) Stone A travels horizontally while falling. (2) Stone B has no horizontal motion. (3) Stone A lands farther from the cliff base than Stone B. (4) But horizontal motion does NOT affect the time of flight. Why the confusion? (1) Many people intuitively think horizontal motion might affect falling time. (2) This is incorrect—horizontal and vertical motions are independent. (3) Horizontal motion doesn't interfere with vertical acceleration. Mathematical proof: Vertical position at time t: y(t) = h - ½gt² (same for both stones) Time when y = 0: 0 = h - ½gt² → t = √(2h/g) (same for both) Horizontal position (Stone A): x(t) = v₀t, where v₀ is horizontal speed (doesn't affect time). Real-world verification: (1) This principle is used in ballistics and projectile motion. (2) Satellites orbit because their horizontal and vertical motions are balanced. (3) Video recordings of simultaneous drops from different heights confirm equal falling times. (4) This principle also explains why a bullet dropped and a bullet fired horizontally from a gun at same height reach the ground simultaneously. Conclusion: The time to reach the ground is determined solely by gravitational force and height. Horizontal velocity is completely independent of vertical motion, so it does NOT affect the time of flight.

questions.question: Explain how a magnet can float above another magnet using repulsive force. Describe what happens to the floating magnet if you push it down gently.
questions.answer: Two magnets with like poles facing repel each other. Repulsive force can balance the weight of the upper magnet, causing it to float. Pushing down increases overlap and increases repulsive force, which pushes back up.
questions.solution: Magnetic levitation demonstrates non-contact repulsive force: Floating mechanism: (1) Two ring magnets are positioned vertically with like poles facing (e.g., North-North). (2) Magnetic force: Like poles repel each other strongly. (3) Repulsive force acts upward on the upper magnet. (4) This repulsive force must exceed the weight of the upper magnet. (5) When repulsive force = weight, the magnet floats in equilibrium. (6) The upper magnet hovers above the lower magnet without touching. Force balance at equilibrium: (1) Gravitational force (weight): Acts downward on upper magnet. (2) Magnetic repulsive force: Acts upward from lower magnet. (3) At equilibrium position: Repulsive force = Weight. (4) Net force = 0, so magnet remains stationary (floats). What happens when pushed down: (1) When gently pushed down, the upper magnet moves closer to the lower magnet. (2) Magnetic force increases dramatically with decreasing distance. (3) As the magnet gets closer, repulsive force increases significantly. (4) Eventually, repulsive force exceeds weight. (5) Net upward force increases. (6) This net upward force pushes the magnet back up. (7) Magnet returns to original equilibrium position. Stability of floating: (1) The equilibrium is stable due to distance-dependent force. (2) If magnet moves down (closer): Force increases → pushes back up. (3) If magnet could move up (farther): Force decreases → falls back down. (4) This automatic adjustment maintains floating. Distance dependence of magnetic force: (1) Magnetic force decreases with distance (not as dramatically as gravity). (2) As distance increases, force decreases. (3) At equilibrium, the specific distance produces exactly the right force to support the weight. (4) If distance is too small: Force > Weight → Magnet pushed up. (5) If distance is too large: Force < Weight → Magnet falls down. Practical implications: (1) Magnetic levitation (maglev) trains use similar principles. (2) Magnetic bearings use repulsive forces to eliminate friction. (3) Stable floating requires precise balance or active stabilization. (4) Demonstrations of magnetic levitation are common in physics education. Requirements for stable floating: (1) Like poles must be aligned (for repulsion). (2) Magnet must be light enough for repulsive force to support. (3) The equilibrium position must be within the range of forces available. (4) Stability is automatic due to force-distance relationship. This demonstrates that non-contact forces like magnetism can produce dramatic effects, including levitation, without any physical contact between objects.

questions.question: A force of 10 N is needed to prevent a moving ball from sliding off a table. Explain what happens to the ball and identify all forces involved.
questions.answer: A 10 N force (likely friction or applied force) opposes the ball's motion. Gravitational force acts downward, normal force balances it. Without the opposing force, the ball slides off due to its inertia.
questions.solution: This scenario involves analyzing balanced and unbalanced forces: Initial situation (ball moving on table): (1) Gravitational force: Acts downward on the ball. (2) Normal force: Acts upward from table surface, balancing gravitational force. (3) Friction force: Acts opposite to the ball's motion (horizontally), gradually slowing it. (4) The ball eventually stops due to friction if given enough time. The applied 10 N force: (1) This force is needed to prevent the ball from sliding off the table. (2) It could be: Applied force from a hand or stick. Friction force at the table edge. (3) This force acts horizontally, perpendicular to the ball's motion toward the edge. (4) It changes the ball's trajectory, preventing it from reaching the edge. Forces in the system: Vertical direction: (1) Gravitational force (downward) = Weight of ball. (2) Normal force (upward) = Weight of ball. (3) These balance, no vertical motion occurs. Horizontal direction (toward table edge): (1) Ball's inertia causes it to move toward the edge. (2) The applied 10 N force acts perpendicular to this motion (or directly opposite). (3) This force changes the direction of the ball's motion. (4) The magnitude (10 N) is sufficient to prevent the ball from reaching the edge. What would happen without the force: (1) Only friction would slow the ball's motion. (2) Depending on table dimensions and ball's speed, the ball might slide off the edge. (3) Once off the edge, gravitational force would pull it downward. (4) The ball would fall to the ground. Why the specific force is needed: (1) 10 N is the exact force needed to alter the ball's path sufficiently. (2) Less than 10 N might be insufficient to prevent sliding off. (3) More than 10 N would be unnecessary but would still prevent sliding. (4) The required force depends on: The ball's mass. The ball's velocity. The distance to the table edge. The direction of motion. Newton's first law relevance: (1) Without any force, the moving ball would continue moving straight (Newton's first law). (2) The 10 N force is needed to overcome inertia and change the ball's direction. (3) This demonstrates that force is required to change an object's state of motion. Practical applications: (1) Sports: Controlling ball direction in various sports requires appropriate forces. (2) Manufacturing: Guiding moving objects on assembly lines requires controlled forces. (3) Safety: Barriers and walls use normal force to prevent objects from proceeding. (4) Vehicles: Steering forces change the direction of moving vehicles.
